title: Fix daemon background startup (pre-existing)
description: FIXED: Daemon would exit immediately when started in background mode because start-daemon would return without blocking. The condition wait loop only executed when foreground=T. Now always blocks until stop-daemon is called, in both modes. Fixes the architectural issue where 'crichton start' would exit immediately, leaving no process running.

title: Add thread synchronization barriers in Discord channel lifecycle
description: Discord channel (channels/discord.lisp) spawns heartbeat and gateway threads but lacks explicit synchronization during shutdown. Race condition possible when stop-flag is checked vs threads still accessing discord-ws. Add condition variables and explicit wait-for-thread-shutdown barriers before returning from stop methods.

title: Refactor host-log-callback for memory safety
description: The host-log-callback (wasm/engine.lisp:164-183) processes WASM function arguments without re-entrancy guards or pointer validation. Refactor to: (1) validate all cffi:mem-aptr accesses, (2) add error handling for malformed memory reads, (3) protect against callback re-entrance while caller context is unstable.

title: Add runtime bounds checks in read-wasm-string callback
description: The read-wasm-string function in wasm/engine.lisp accesses WASM linear memory without proper pre-flight validation. Add explicit bounds checking before each memory access to prevent segfaults from malformed WASM modules or corrupted argument pointers.

title: SHOWSTOPPER: Memory faults in SBCL daemon process
description:
The daemon process is experiencing memory faults/corruption errors from SBCL. This is a critical stability issue that makes the daemon unusable.

Symptoms:
- Memory faults appearing in journalctl logs
- SBCL corruption warnings
- Process instability

Potential causes to investigate:
1. FFI/CFFI issues with wasmtime bindings
2. Thread safety issues (bordeaux-threads, websocket-driver)
3. Unsafe memory operations
4. Foreign function callbacks (host-log-callback in wasm/engine.lisp)
5. WebSocket library (websocket-driver) memory management

Investigation steps:
1. Check journalctl for specific error messages and stack traces
2. Review all FFI/CFFI code for safety
3. Check thread synchronization primitives
4. Review foreign callbacks for proper memory management
5. Consider running under valgrind or SBCL debug mode

This blocks production use of the daemon.
close_reason: Resolved by microkernel architecture (cricht-j38): daemon loads fresh via systemd, no save-lisp-and-die, no stale cl+ssl pointers.

comments.author: Gordon J. Broom
comments.text: Root cause found: `call *0x40(%rax)` in libssl.so.3 — OpenSSL vtable dispatch with NULL method pointer. cl+ssl bakes stale global context/method pointers into save-lisp-and-die image. Attempted fixes (cl+ssl:reload, sb-ext:*init-hooks*) insufficient. Superseded by cricht-j38: microkernel architecture eliminates save-lisp-and-die for the daemon entirely.

title: SHOWSTOPPER: Daemon crashes on startup with SWANK-CLIENT::CONNECTION-LOCK error
description:
The daemon immediately dies upon startup with an error:

  There is no applicable method for the generic function
  #<STANDARD-GENERIC-FUNCTION SWANK-CLIENT::CONNECTION-LOCK (1)>
  when called with arguments (NIL).

This appears to be in cli/remote.lisp where swank-client:slime-close is called with a NIL connection in the unwind-protect clause.

The issue occurs when:
1. remote-eval-quiet tries to connect to Swank
2. Connection fails (daemon not fully started yet)
3. unwind-protect calls (swank-client:slime-close connection) with connection=NIL
4. swank-client tries to access CONNECTION-LOCK on NIL, crashes

Fix: Check if connection is non-NIL before calling slime-close:
  (when connection
    (swank-client:slime-close connection))

This is blocking daemon startup and must be fixed immediately.
close_reason: Fixed by adding nil check before swank-client:slime-close

title: Fix event listener leak in OfficeHoursManager
description: _createCleanupHandler creates new function references instead of using the ones added via addEventListener, so removeEventListener never works. Handlers accumulate each time office-hours mode is entered. Fix by passing actual handler references into the cleanup function.

title: Add input validation and sanitization
description: Implement comprehensive input validation for all user inputs to prevent injection attacks and data corruption. Key areas: StorageService (faculty name), parser.js (parseTimeToMinutes should throw/return null for invalid input), all data from chrome.storage before use. Validate format, length, and allowed characters. Sanitize before storage and before DOM insertion.

title: Add try-catch blocks to all async functions

Key files: content.js (generateTimetable, updatePageState), popup.js (checkPageStatus, extractTimetable), CourseFilter.js (all async methods), OfficeHoursManager.js. Ensure errors are surfaced to users appropriately rather than failing silently.

title: Fix MutationObserver memory leak
description: The MutationObserver in content.js is never disconnected, causing a memory leak. Add cleanup handler to disconnect observer when page unloads or extension is disabled. Implement proper lifecycle management: store observer reference, add beforeunload handler to call observer.disconnect().

description: Analyze the mycamosun-timetable codebase to understand current functionality, identify areas for improvement, and document the architecture. This will serve as a foundation for future AI-assisted development sessions.

title: Streaming SSE read-timeout not firing on hung connections
description:
## Root Cause

The Anthropic streaming connection stalls mid-response (or never starts). Nothing terminates it reliably because:

1. **SO_RCVTIMEO** (dexador :read-timeout 120) is per-syscall, not a budget. While data trickles in — even one byte every 119s — the timeout never fires.
2. **with-timeout's bt:interrupt-thread** is unreliable for threads blocked in OpenSSL SSL_read. The SIGUSR2 signal may be delivered, but SBCL or OpenSSL retries the syscall transparently before running the interrupt function.

The hung thread holds the session claim indefinitely, blocking all further requests on that session from every client.

## What Actually Happened Today (cascade from this bug)

Each symptom below was only visible once the previous was patched. All of them trace back to the session claim being held by a thread that cannot be unblocked.

**1. :NULL session deadlock** (fixed — rpc/protocol.lisp)
shasht deserialises JSON null as the Lisp keyword :NULL, which is truthy. (or :NULL (create-session)) skipped session creation, so every new-session request landed on a permanent :NULL slot. When that hung, all new sessions were blocked.

**2. Torn history on rollback** (fixed — agent/loop.lisp)
initialize-messages used nconc to append the user message onto the snapshot list, mutating it in place. The unwind-protect in handle-streaming-chat-request restored the mutated snapshot (which by then included a partial assistant message), not the original. Fixed: switched to append.

**3. Empty SSE stream committed as valid response** (fixed — llm/anthropic.lisp)
When the SSE stream returned immediate EOF (stale pooled connection), stream-state-result returned :content nil. run-agent-loop appended this nil-content assistant message and returned successfully — committing it to session history. Every subsequent request then hit 'message missing :content'. Fixed: stream-state-result now errors on empty blocks.

**4. Connection pool reuse of dead sockets** (fixed — llm/anthropic.lisp)
Dexador's LRU pool was handing out CLOSE_WAIT connections to new streaming requests. Fixed: :use-connection-pool nil on the streaming POST.

**5. Watchdog destroy-thread race** (fixed — skills/retry-infrastructure.lisp)
If bt:destroy-thread failed silently (swallowed by ignore-errors), the watchdog kept sleeping and fired later against whatever the thread happened to be doing — causing spurious cancellations on innocent requests. Fixed: cancellation flag checked before firing.

## Assessment of Fixes Made

The fixes are individually correct, but several compensate for symptoms rather than the root cause:

- :use-connection-pool nil prevents reuse of dead connections but does not explain why those connections ended up broken in the pool. Root cause: interrupted streams should close the underlying fd before returning, not leave it for the pool to discover later.
- stream-state-result erroring on empty blocks catches one failure mode (immediate EOF) but not a related one: partial response (some blocks received, then connection dropped without a message-stop event). That case silently succeeds with truncated content.
- with-timeout + cancellation flag is correct hygiene but does not solve the core problem — interrupt-thread remains unreliable for SSL reads.
- normalize-messages now reports which message is bad — diagnostic only.

## The Actual Fix Required

Replace interrupt-thread in the streaming path with stream-closing. The watchdog should hold a reference to response-stream and call (close response-stream :abort t) on timeout. This closes the underlying fd directly, which is guaranteed to unblock SSL_read regardless of signal delivery. The error propagates through process-sse-stream → stream-message → with-llm-error-handling → clean error response to client.

Implementation site: process-sse-stream in llm/anthropic.lisp. The watchdog needs to be co-located with the stream so it can close it. The with-timeout wrapper at the agent-loop level remains as belt-and-suspenders for the non-streaming path and tool execution.

## Post-Fix Consolidation Needed

1. Two timeout mechanisms (with-timeout in run-agent-loop + stream-closing in process-sse-stream) cover what should be one concept. After the fix, document clearly which does what and why both are kept.
2. stream-state-result should also detect partial state: blocks received but no message-stop event (dropped mid-stream). Currently this silently returns truncated content.
3. The snapshot/rollback invariant in handle-streaming-chat-request is fragile and non-obvious. The comment about copy-list was wrong (it protects *chat-sessions* but not the snapshot variable). Consider whether run-agent-loop should own its message list from the start rather than depending on callers not to mutate what they pass in.
4. initialize-messages now documents that append is essential for rollback safety — but this is a coupling between loop.lisp and rpc-server.lisp. The right long-term fix may be to make run-agent-loop copy its input unconditionally so callers do not need to know about this constraint.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A stalled SSE stream (partial response then silence) times out within the configured interval without SWANK intervention
- Timed-out requests surface a clean error to the client; session history is intact for retry
- No session claim is held after a timeout — clients can retry immediately
- The post-fix consolidation items above are addressed in a follow-up pass
close_reason: Stream-closing watchdog implemented in process-sse-stream; consolidation pass complete. Committed 770ba24.

title: Store new RSS articles to inbox; drop per-item notifications
description: Modify rss-monitor-poll in rss-monitor.lisp. Currently: logs each new article title via log:info (noise) and fires a user notification for every new-item batch (interrupting). Change to: (1) call inbox-insert-article for each new item found, (2) log the count as log:info heartbeat only — 'N new items in Feed' — no notification fired. The notification pipeline is replaced by morning-briefing. The per-item title log:info lines are removed entirely. Depends on article inbox being in place.

title: Add SQLite article inbox for RSS monitoring
description: New file rss-inbox.lisp. RSS monitoring currently discards articles after mark-seen — there's nowhere to accumulate them for later review or scoring. Add a SQLite-backed article inbox following the books.lisp pattern. Schema: guid (PK), feed_name, title, link, description, pub_date, first_seen (unix timestamp), interest_score (real nullable), scored_at (integer nullable), saved_to_raindrop (integer 0/1 default 0), reviewed (integer 0/1 default 0). Functions: inbox-insert-article (idempotent on guid), inbox-get-unscored, inbox-get-unsaved (scored >= threshold, not yet saved), inbox-get-by-feed (unreviewed items for a given feed name), inbox-get-saved-since (for morning briefing), inbox-mark-saved, inbox-mark-reviewed, inbox-stats. DB file lives alongside other Crichton data files.

title: Fix ETYPECASE in RSS parsing when feed body is byte vector
description: xmls:parse has an etypecase that only accepts string or stream. When dexador's charset detection fails (e.g., a feed with invalid UTF-8 bytes despite claiming UTF-8 in Content-Type), decode-body catches a babel:character-decoding-error and falls back to returning the raw byte vector. That vector hits xmls:parse's etypecase, causing the error. Fix: add ensure-xml-string helper in fetch-feed that decodes byte vectors using UTF-8 with ISO-8859-1 fallback.
close_reason: Fixed in 59040b8: added ensure-xml-string helper in fetch-feed

title: Fix runaway agent: timeout swallowed during streaming + no tool timeout
description:
Two bugs cause agent requests to run indefinitely (observed: >30min on 'Mute the JeeLabs feed'):

(1) dispatch-sse-event in anthropic.lisp catches (error (c) ...) broadly, which silently swallows the bt:interrupt-thread signal that with-timeout uses. The 300s LLM API timeout fires but the error never escapes the SSE loop, so streaming continues until Anthropic finishes.

(2) execute-tool-calls in agent/loop.lisp has no timeout at all. A hanging tool (e.g. persist-rss-monitors blocking on flush-all-storage) blocks the agent loop indefinitely.

Fix: (a) define operation-cancelled condition in retry-infrastructure.lisp and export it; (b) change with-timeout to signal operation-cancelled via bt:interrupt-thread; (c) change dispatch-sse-event to specifically re-raise operation-cancelled instead of swallowing it; (d) wrap execute-tool-calls in with-timeout (separate configurable limit, default 60s).

title: Fix session-busy error on reconnect after C-c
description:
After C-c in the chat window, crichton-reconnect, and crichton-chat produce 'Session is busy -- a previous request is still processing.' The session claim is never released until the in-flight LLM call completes naturally (up to 120-300s), because rpc-push swallows write errors so the streaming callback never propagates the disconnect.

Fix: (1) add a claim timestamp + token to *chat-session-active* so session-claim force-releases stale claims (>60s) and session-release is a no-op if the token doesn't match the current holder; (2) make the streaming delta lambda in handle-streaming-chat-request write directly instead of via rpc-push, so a closed-socket write error propagates out of run-agent/stream and triggers the unwind-protect immediately.

title: Session lock held across entire LLM call; client disconnect does not release it
description:
handle-streaming-chat-request holds the per-session bt:with-lock-held for the full duration of run-agent/stream. If the client disconnects mid-flight, the agent thread keeps running and keeps the lock. On reconnect with the same session-id the new request blocks on the same lock and hangs indefinitely showing [thinking...].

Fix: detect client disconnect and interrupt the agent thread, or restructure so the session lock is not held across the LLM network call (only held when reading/writing *chat-sessions*).

title: FEAT/BUG: context window overflow protection — history truncation and tool result capping
description: Fixed 'prompt is too long' errors caused by accumulated session history and large tool results. (1) truncate-history in agent/loop.lisp: drops oldest user/assistant message pairs until total character count is under 300K-char budget. Allows dropping all history if necessary. (2) cap-tool-result: truncates individual tool results at 100K chars before storing in message history, appending a notice. Both applied in run-agent and run-agent/stream. Root cause of original incident: a 2MB JSON file read via read_file was stored in session history and replayed on every subsequent request. Commit: 56b40ab.

title: BUG: emacsql double-quote encoding broke all org-roam DB queries
description: The org-roam SQLite database stores all string values with surrounding double-quotes (emacsql serialization format, e.g. tag stored as '"programming"' not 'programming'). This caused: (1) list_tags always returning empty — truename failed on quoted paths so om-filter-by-allowed-paths discarded every row; (2) exact-match queries (tag=, node id=, todo state=, priority=, NOT IN for done keywords, link type=) silently returning no results because the unquoted bind parameter never matched the quoted stored value. LIKE-based title search accidentally worked because substring matching sees through the surrounding quotes. Fixed by adding om-unquote-string (applied in om-sql-rows-to-plists to strip quotes from all returned strings) and om-db-string (applied at every exact-match bind-parameter call site). Commit: 2734c55.

title: Skill parameter passing and pipeline composition
description:
invoke-skill currently passes :args nil to WASM — no way to pass parameters to skills. Skills like rss-filter are pure functions that need input data (items + keywords) but the invocation API provides none.

## Problem

1. invoke-skill signature is (name &key entry-point) — no params argument
2. The WASM JSON ABI (run-wasm-bytes-json-call) already supports structured parameter passing through shared memory, but invoke-skill doesn't use it
3. The agent tool handler for 'invoke' action also passes no parameters
4. No mechanism to wire skill outputs into other skill inputs (pipelines)

## Design

### Phase 1: Parameter Passing

Extend invoke-skill to accept :params and route to JSON ABI:

  (defun invoke-skill (name &key entry-point params abi) ...)

- :params (Lisp object) — JSON-serializable plist/hash-table
- :abi — :auto (default), :json, or :i32
  - :auto rules: if params non-nil => JSON ABI; else if manifest declares JSON ABI => JSON ABI with {}; else legacy i32 path
- Backwards compatible: zero-arg skills keep working unchanged

### Phase 2: Manifest Schema Extension

Extend [provides] in skill.toml:

  [provides.function.filter_items]
  entry_point = "filter_items"
  abi = "json"
  description = "Filter RSS items by keywords"
  input_type = "crichton/rss-items+keywords@1"
  output_type = "crichton/rss-filter-result@1"

### Phase 3: Pipeline Executor

Add skills/pipeline.lisp with:
- Pipeline data structure: ordered list of steps with id, kind (:wasm/:builtin/:auto), skill, entry-point, params
- Reference mechanism: (:ref "steps.fetch.items") to wire outputs to inputs
- execute-pipeline function that iterates steps, resolves refs, invokes skills, stores results
- Works for both WASM and built-in skills via unified dispatch

### Phase 4: Agent Tool Exposure

Extend the skills agent tool to support:
- 'invoke' action with entry_point and params fields
- 'pipeline' action to run pipeline specs from the agent

## Example Pipeline

  rss-fetch (built-in) --items--> rss-filter (WASM) --matches--> pushover-notify (built-in)

## Security

- Each pipeline step runs under its own manifest-derived skill-context
- Cap serialized JSON input size per call (configurable)
- Enforce existing max-memory-mb and max-cpu-seconds from capabilities
- Treat pipeline composition as trusted-daemon logic
notes: Step 1 (parameter passing) implemented in commit 1360788. invoke-skill now accepts :params and :abi keywords. Steps 2-4 remain.
close_reason: All 4 phases complete: (1) invoke-skill :params/:abi, (2) manifest-function-* accessors, (3) pipeline executor with ref resolution and builtin registry, (4) skills tool pipeline action. rss-filter WASM skill works end-to-end.

comments.author: Gordon J. Broom
comments.text: Phase 1 complete and verified end-to-end. WASM panic fixed (missing wasm_import_module annotation). rss-filter skill invocation works with JSON parameter passing. Phases 2-4 (manifest schema, pipeline executor, agent tool exposure) remain open.

title: Skill loader ignores manifest (skill.toml)
description:
The skill loading mechanism does not read the manifest (skill.toml) for:

1. **WASM file name**: The manifest declares metadata.wasm_file but the loader doesn't read it. This prevents customizing WASM output filenames (e.g., Cargo produces rss_filter.wasm but manifest says skill.wasm).

2. **Entry point function names**: The manifest declares provides.functions array but the loader doesn't read it. This forces hardcoded function name expectations.

**Impact:**
- Manifest declarations are ignored
- Build output must match hardcoded loader expectations
- Defeats the purpose of having a declarative manifest

**Expected behavior:**
The skill loader should:
1. Parse skill.toml manifest
2. Extract metadata.wasm_file to determine which WASM file to load
3. Extract provides.functions to determine which exported functions to call
4. Use these manifest values instead of hardcoded assumptions

**Location:** The bug is in the skill loader code (likely daemon/skill-loader.lisp or similar).

**Workarounds:** Rename compiled WASM file to match loader's hardcoded expectations, or ensure Cargo package name produces the expected output filename.

title: Prompt injection mitigation across all LLM surfaces
description: Crichton has multiple surfaces where untrusted input flows into LLM prompts: (1) Discord channel messages fed to the agent loop, (2) RSS feed content processed by the agent, (3) tool results returned to the LLM, (4) Amp orchestrator prompts (covered separately in cricht-ex8). An attacker could craft input that hijacks the agent's system prompt, causes it to invoke dangerous tools, leak credentials, or exfiltrate data. Defence-in-depth needed: (a) input sanitization layer before any user/external text enters a prompt, (b) clear system/user message boundaries in the agent loop, (c) tool allowlists per input channel (e.g. Discord users shouldn't trigger credential operations), (d) output filtering to prevent the LLM from returning secrets, (e) rate limiting on tool invocations to bound damage, (f) audit logging of all prompts and tool calls. This is a systemic concern that touches agent/loop.lisp, channels/discord.lisp, skills/builtins/rss.lisp, and the amp orchestrator.

title: RSS Filter: Extend WASM runner for JSON-through-memory ABI
description: The current WASM runner (wasm/engine.lisp, runner/server.lisp) only supports i32 args and i32 returns. The rss-filter skill uses a JSON-through-linear-memory ABI: the host must call the skill's exported alloc() to allocate input buffer space, write JSON params into WASM linear memory, call filter_items(params_ptr, params_len, out_ptr, out_len) -> i32, then read JSON result back from the output buffer. Add a run-wasm-json-call (or similar) that: (1) serializes a Lisp plist to JSON, (2) calls exported alloc to get input buffer, (3) copies JSON into WASM memory, (4) allocates output buffer via alloc, (5) calls the target function, (6) reads JSON result from WASM memory, (7) calls dealloc to free buffers. This is the bridge between Crichton's Lisp world and Rust WASM skills that exchange structured data.

title: Microkernel architecture: split daemon and client, unified RPC
description:
Redesign Crichton to eliminate save-lisp-and-die for the daemon, splitting into:

1. Daemon (kernel) - started via SBCL command line in systemd unit. Loads system fresh each time, no stale foreign pointers. Exposes a single NDJSON/Unix-socket RPC endpoint for all user-space communication. Swank remains internal for developer access only.

2. crichton-client - thin chat-only binary (or script). Connects to daemon via the RPC socket, drops straight into a conversation REPL. No management commands, no Swank visibility.

3. Unified RPC protocol - the existing NDJSON protocol (rpc/protocol.lisp) becomes the single interface between kernel and user space. Chat, downloaded WASM skills, and channel adapters all use the same socket/protocol.

4. Management via systemd - start/stop/status moves to systemctl. CLI management commands are removed.

Motivation: Eliminates the entire class of save-lisp-and-die stale foreign pointer bugs (cricht-bt3). Clean kernel/user-space separation. Single protocol for all external communication.

Supersedes cricht-bt3 (memory faults become impossible without saved images).
notes:
## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: RPC chat endpoint in daemon
- Add a daemon-side RPC server listening on ~/.crichton/daemon.sock
- Add chat op: receives text, routes through agent loop, returns response
- Support conversation state (session ID in request)
- Reuse existing NDJSON protocol from rpc/protocol.lisp

### Phase 2: systemd unit for fresh-load daemon
- Create crichton.service that runs sbcl --eval to load and start
- Wrapper shell script for the ExecStart command
- SIGTERM handler already exists in lifecycle.lisp
- Test start/stop/restart lifecycle

### Phase 3: crichton-client chat binary
- Minimal save-lisp-and-die binary: only usocket + shasht (no cl+ssl, no wasmtime)
- Connects to ~/.crichton/daemon.sock
- Interactive REPL: prompt, send chat op, display response
- Or: simple shell script using socat/ncat for zero dependencies
- Handle daemon-not-running gracefully

### Phase 4: Migrate channels to RPC (optional, later)
- Discord adapter connects via RPC socket instead of in-process
- Same protocol as skills and client
- Can run as separate process for fault isolation

### Phase 5: Cleanup
- Remove old CLI management commands (start/stop/status)
- Remove save-lisp-and-die build from AGENTS.md
- Remove cl+ssl init hooks (no longer needed)
- Update AGENT_SPEC.md and README
- Close cricht-bt3
close_reason: All 5 phases implemented: daemon RPC server, systemd unit, crichton-client binary, channels via RPC, cleanup.

title: flush-all-storage holds storage-lock for entire flush, starving other persistence ops
description:
flush-all-storage acquires storage-lock once and holds it for the entire flush of all stores. When any individual store is large (e.g. RSS inbox with items from 174 monitors), this starves every other operation that needs storage-lock — including rss-monitor-stop, persist-rss-monitors, and persist-user-tasks — for the full duration of the flush.

Observed: task:storage-sync held storage-lock for several minutes while flushing a large RSS inbox, causing tool/rss to block on storage-lock (holding *rss-monitors-lock*), which in turn blocked all persist-rss-monitors calls.

Fix options:
- Dirty-flag individual stores so only modified stores are flushed each cycle
- Flush one store at a time, releasing and reacquiring storage-lock between stores
- Separate the RSS inbox store from the main storage flush cycle given its potential size

title: Add 'make rebuild' target and document pre-restart build step
description:
After this session we discovered that restarting the daemon after relying purely on hot-loading left all 174 RSS monitors in backoff because the ETYPECASE fix (commit 59040b8) had only been hot-loaded — not recompiled into the ASDF fasl cache before restart.

In practice the fasls were already up to date (compiled Apr 13 via the last make run), so this time no stale code was loaded. But the workflow gap is real: if someone restarts the daemon without running make, they could pick up stale fasls from a previous session. There is also no documentation warning about this.

What needs to be done:
1. Add a Makefile target (e.g. 'rebuild-daemon') that force-recompiles the crichton system into the ASDF cache: sbcl --eval '(asdf:load-system :crichton :force t)' and exits. This gives a cheap one-step way to bake hot-loaded changes before a restart.
2. Add a note to AGENTS.org under 'Daemon Development' / 'Hot-loading' warning that hot-loading updates the running image but the ASDF cache may lag; run 'make rebuild-daemon' before restarting the service.
3. Optionally: wire 'make rebuild-daemon' into the session-close protocol in CLAUDE.md alongside git push.
close_reason: Added make rebuild-daemon target, AGENTS.org fasl cache warning, and CLAUDE.md session-close note

title: Morning briefing should deliver via Pushover
description:
## Problem
The scheduled morning briefing (rss-morning-briefing in skills/builtins/rss-curator.lisp) delivers via notification-post, which pushes to connected TUI subscribers only. If no TUI session is open when the briefing fires, it silently drops. User missed their briefing today because no session was active overnight.

## Solution
Replace or supplement the TUI notification with a Pushover delivery. The pushover-report skill already exists (crichton/skills:pushover-report, also exposed as a tool via define-tool pushover in agent/tools-data.lisp). The morning briefing should call it directly instead of relying on a connected subscriber.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Morning briefing arrives via Pushover regardless of whether a TUI session is open
- The pushover message content matches what the TUI notification text currently contains

title: Add on-demand 'what's new in X' RSS inbox query

Expose via RPC so the TUI/client can ask 'what's new in Hackaday'. Format function rss-inbox-report for display. Allows the user to ask reactively rather than waiting for morning briefing. Depends on article inbox.

Formats a notification: 'Morning briefing: N articles curated — [Title (Feed)] x N'. If nothing was curated, stays silent. Fires a single notification via notification-post with kind='rss-briefing'. Tracks last-briefing-timestamp in storage so restarts don't re-send. Depends on interest scorer being in place and populating saved_to_raindrop.

title: Implement LLM-based interest scorer with Raindrop save
description: The core curation engine — new file rss-curator.lisp. Scheduled task (configurable interval, default every 2h) that: (1) pulls unscored articles from inbox in batches of ~20, (2) calls LLM with interests profile + article batch requesting structured JSON output: array of {guid, score (0.0-1.0), tags [list of strings], reason (one sentence)}, (3) writes scores/tags/reasons back to inbox, (4) for articles with score >= threshold (default 0.7, configurable), calls raindrop-find-or-create-collection 'crichton' then raindrop-create with title/URL/excerpt/tags, marks inbox row saved_to_raindrop=1. raindrop-find-or-create-collection: call raindrop-all-collections, find by title='crichton', create if absent, cache the ID. Score threshold and batch size in config. Depends on: article inbox, interests profile, existing raindrop skill.

title: Add interests profile storage and RPC
description: The interest scorer needs a user-defined interests document to score articles against. Implement storage and retrieval of a free-text interests profile (e.g., 'embedded systems, Rust, Lisp, home automation, photography, woodworking, ...'). Store via the existing storage layer under namespace 'rss', key 'interests-profile'. Expose via RPC handlers: get-interests-profile (returns the text), set-interests-profile (takes new text). Also add a simple get-interests-profile Lisp function used internally by the scorer. The profile is intentionally free-text, not structured — the LLM handles interpretation.

title: Agent loop hangs indefinitely if Claude API call never returns
description:
The LLM HTTP call in the agent loop has no read timeout. If the API call hangs (e.g. after returning a large tool result that triggers a slow or stalled Claude response), the Crichton prompt never returns. Observed: opml_import of a large OPML file registered all monitors successfully but the session hung for 7+ hours afterward.

Fix: add a read timeout to the LLM API call, or wrap the agent loop iteration in with-timeout. Should surface a clean error rather than hanging forever.

Fixed: crichton-connect now unconditionally clears session-id before connecting; crichton-disconnect clears it unconditionally; added crichton-reconnect command that explicitly preserves session-id for intentional resume after daemon restart. title: TUI renders raw ANSI escape codes instead of colours
description:
The TUI chat viewport is stripping the ESC (0x1b) byte from ANSI SGR sequences but leaving the bracket and parameters visible as literal text. For example, what should render as coloured text like a cyan "28h" instead shows as:

  [36m28h[0m

This means the ESC character is being dropped somewhere in the rendering pipeline (likely in tui:render-markdown, viewport content handling, or the chat entry rendering) while the CSI parameters and terminator are passed through as plain text.

The LLM response text itself contains valid ANSI escape sequences (ESC[36m etc.), which cl-tuition's terminal output should pass through. The stripping likely happens during viewport content storage/retrieval or markdown rendering.

Repro: ask the daemon 'show a system dashboard' — the response contains markdown with inline styling that render-markdown converts to ANSI sequences, but they display as raw bracket codes in the viewport.
notes:
## Investigation (2026-03-22) — Static Analysis Complete, Inconclusive

### Root cause: NOT FOUND through static analysis.

Every code path has been read and traced. ESC (char 27) appears to be
preserved at every stage. Runtime diagnostic required.

### Full pipeline traced (all appear correct):

1. **Anthropic SSE → text delta**: shasht decodes `\u001b` → ESC correctly
   (shasht/src/read.lisp lines 139-148: `\uXXXX` decoded via `code-char`)

2. **agent/loop.lisp `run-agent/stream`**: delta passed directly to on-delta
   callback as Lisp string — no transformation

3. **rpc-server.lisp**: delta put in hash-table, `crichton/rpc:write-message`
   serializes via shasht which encodes ESC → `\u001b` in JSON

4. **Socket stream**: `:external-format :utf-8` — ESC is valid single-byte UTF-8

5. **tui/protocol.lisp `read-message`**: `read-line` → `shasht:read-json` decodes
   `\u001b` back to ESC

6. **tui/client.lisp `update-message daemon-chat-delta-msg`**: `concatenate 'string`
   appends delta to entry-text — no transformation

7. **tui/render.lisp `render-chat-entry%`**: calls `tui:render-markdown` (generates
   ANSI codes from markdown). Does NOT call render-styled on the body.

8. **cl-tuition/markdown.lisp `render-markdown`**: `parse-inline-styles` →
   `style-text` → `render-styled` generates ANSI codes. ESC in ESC[...m tokens
   appears correctly preserved through `markdown-wrap-text`/`wrap-text`.

9. **cl-tuition/style.lisp `%tokenize`**: correctly identifies ESC-starting tokens
   as `:ansi` type and preserves full text including ESC. `wrap-text` calls
   `add-text` which does `concatenate 'string` — ESC preserved.

10. **cl-tuition/style.lisp `render-styled`**: searches for `ESC[0m` (rst) and
    re-applies outer style after inner resets. Appears correct.

11. **cl-tuition/layout.lisp `join-vertical`**: uses `visible-length` for padding
    calc but `format nil "~A~A" text pad-str` for output — ESC preserved.

12. **cl-tuition/components/viewport.lisp**: `viewport-set-content` calls
    `split-string-by-newline` (preserves ESC). `viewport-view` uses
    `format s "~A" line` — ESC preserved.

13. **cl-tuition/src/renderer.lisp `render`**: `write-string view-string stream`
    — ESC preserved.

### One confirmed ESC-stripper — but wrong location:
`agent/sanitize.lisp %strip-control-chars` strips ESC (char 27 < Space 32).
BUT this is only called on USER INPUT before sending to the LLM, not on
LLM responses. Not the cause.

### Known good clarification:
The ANSI codes are NOT from the LLM response directly. They are GENERATED
by `tui:render-markdown` processing markdown (bold, code spans etc.) in
`render-chat-entry%`. So the pipeline is:
  LLM markdown text → render-markdown → ANSI-coded string → viewport → terminal

### Potential runtime suspects not ruleable-out statically:

A. **viewport horizontal-scroll path** (viewport.lisp lines 133-142):
   uses `tuition:visible-length` (skips ANSI) as character index for
   `subseq` — this is a confirmed bug for ANSI strings when x > 0, but
   only triggers with horizontal scrolling (x-offset > 0). May not be
   the main cause.

B. **%tokenize stops ESC scan at `m`** (style.lisp ~line 769-776):
   Only scans `ESC...m` for ANSI tokens. Sequences ending in other
   final bytes (e.g. `ESC[?25l`) would scan past the final byte until
   the next `m` in content — could mangle content. Not confirmed to
   cause ESC loss for standard SGR sequences.

C. **SBCL stream/external-format issue at runtime**: Not ruleable-out
   without running the code.

### Diagnostic tool created:
`tests/diagnostic-u7n.lisp` — load into running TUI via SLIME port 4006.
Functions:
  `(crichton/diag-u7n:run)` — tests stages 1-8 plus tokenizer, outputs
    "ESC present: YES/NO" and char codes at each stage
  `(crichton/diag-u7n:run-tui-stage)` — tests `render-chat-entry%` live
    in the running TUI model

### Next step:
Connect SLIME to TUI Swank (port 4006), load diagnostic, run it.
The first stage that flips from "ESC present: YES" to "NO" is the bug.

title: rss-filter skill fails to run: no parameter passing in invoke-skill
description:
## Bug

Invoking the rss-filter WASM skill fails because invoke-skill has no mechanism to pass parameters. The rss-filter skill is a pure function (entry point: filter_items) that expects structured JSON input (items array + keywords array), but invoke-skill calls run-wasm-bytes-with-host-fns with :args nil.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy rss-filter skill to ~/.crichton/skills/rss-filter/
2. Run (crichton/skills:invoke-skill "rss-filter")
3. The skill's filter_items function receives no input data

## Expected Behavior

Should be able to call:
  (invoke-skill "rss-filter"
    :entry-point "filter_items"
    :params (list :items items :keywords '("Carney" "Bank of Canada") :match-mode "any"))

## Actual Behavior

invoke-skill signature is (name &key entry-point) — no :params keyword. The WASM function is called with :args nil and either crashes or returns empty results.

## Root Cause

invoke-skill in skills/registry.lisp line 228 calls:
  (crichton/wasm:run-wasm-bytes-with-host-fns wasm-bytes entry-point :args nil :nresults 1)

This uses the i32 ABI, not the JSON ABI. The JSON ABI (run-wasm-bytes-json-call) exists in wasm/engine.lisp but invoke-skill doesnt use it.

## Fix

Blocked on cricht-u1x (skill parameter passing feature). Once :params support is added to invoke-skill, rss-filter will work as designed in RSS-FILTER-SKILL.org.
close_reason: Fixed: rss-filter WASM binary had no import section for env.log. Added #[link(wasm_import_module = "env")] to Rust source. Skill now invokes successfully with JSON parameter passing.

title: Amp orchestrator: prompt injection mitigation
description: The amp-orchestrator skill passes user-supplied descriptions and context directly into prompts sent to the Amp CLI. A malicious or compromised upstream input (e.g. from an RSS feed piped through the agent, or a Discord message) could inject instructions that cause Amp to perform unintended actions (delete files, exfiltrate data, etc.). Defence-in-depth needed: (1) sanitize/escape user-supplied strings before embedding in prompts, (2) structured prompt templates with clear system/user boundaries, (3) review/confirmation step for destructive operations, (4) restrict Amp working directory and permissions via CLI flags if available, (5) log full prompts sent to Amp for audit trail.

Enforce limits: MAX_ITEMS=1000, MAX_KEYWORDS=100, MAX_KEYWORD_LEN=200, MAX_ITEM_TEXT_LEN=100000. validate_params() should return errors for oversized inputs before any processing. See RSS-FILTER-SKILL.org §Input Validation.

title: RSS Filter: Implement filter_items WASM entry point
description: Implement the core filter_items function in examples/skills/rss-filter/src/lib.rs. Includes: FilterParams/RssItem/MatchedItem/FilterResult/Statistics structs with serde derive, check_item_match() logic for keyword matching with any/all modes, case-sensitive/insensitive matching, multi-field search (title/description/content), alloc/dealloc FFI exports, log_debug host function import. Note: the host log function follows the meaning-of-life convention (env.log with level/ptr/len signature). See RSS-FILTER-SKILL.org §Rust WASM Implementation.
notes:
IMPORTANT ABI CONTEXT: The current WASM runner (run-wasm-with-host-fns / run-wasm-bytes-with-host-fns) only passes i32 args and returns i32 results. The rss-filter skill uses a JSON-through-linear-memory ABI: filter_items(params_ptr, params_len, out_ptr, out_len) -> i32. The Rust skill exports alloc/dealloc for memory management. The host function for logging is env.log with signature (i32 level, i32 ptr, i32 len) -> () — matching the existing host-log-callback in wasm/engine.lisp. The skill must import 'env' 'log' (not 'env' 'log_debug' as the RSS-FILTER-SKILL.org spec says). Align with the actual Crichton host function interface.
The Rust extern "C" import must be: (import "env" "log" (func (param i32 i32 i32))) matching the host-log-callback in wasm/engine.lisp. The spec's 'log_debug' name is wrong — use the standard env.log(level, ptr, len) interface. Level 0=debug, 1=info, 2=warn, 3=error.

title: Convert defstruct to defclass for hot-reload safety
description:
SBCL rejects incompatible struct redefinitions during hot-reload, but CLOS classes handle slot changes gracefully via update-instance-for-redefined-class. Since Crichton's primary development workflow is hot-loading via Swank, all mutable data structures should use defclass.

Structs to convert:
- scheduled-task (scheduler.lisp) — already hit this during hot-reload
- agent-tool (agent/tools.lisp)
- skill-context (runner/skill-context.lisp)
- meter, usage-record (token-usage.lisp)
- stopwatch (timing.lisp)
- feed-item (rss.lisp)
- channel-message (channels/protocol.lisp)

For each conversion:
1. Replace defstruct with defclass
2. Convert slot definitions to CLOS syntax (:initarg, :accessor, :initform, :type)
3. Update constructors (make-instance or custom wrapper)
4. Update all slot accessors at call sites
5. Verify hot-reload works with live instances

Priority: scheduled-task, agent-tool, skill-context (most likely to evolve). The rest can follow.

title: Add skill-builder skill, like in OpenClaw
HOOBS home automation skill
description:
Built-in skill for interacting with a local HOOBS (hoobs.com) home automation hub via its REST API (https://hoobs.com/documentation/developer-api-reference/).

HOOBS is a Homebridge-based smart home hub that bridges smart accessories to Apple Home, Google Home, and Alexa. It exposes a comprehensive REST API at http://<host>:80.

API capabilities to expose:
- Authentication: POST /api/auth/login (username/password → token). Token used in Authorization header for all subsequent requests.
- Accessories: GET /api/accessories (list rooms + accessories), GET /api/accessories/:id (single accessory), GET /api/accessories/:id/characteristics (read state), PUT /api/accessories/:id (control: set characteristic values — lights on/off, brightness, thermostat target temp, lock/unlock, etc.).
- Rooms: GET /api/rooms (list rooms), PUT /api/room/:id (control all devices in a room, e.g. all-off).
- Service status: GET /api/service (hub + bridge status).
- System info: GET /api/system (CPU, memory, temp, disk of the HOOBS device itself).

Implementation:
- Daemon-side built-in (like weather, RSS) — runs inside the TCB, not in WASM.
- Store credentials in the age-encrypted credential store as 'hoobs' with fields :host, :port (default 80), :username, :password.
- Session management: authenticate on first use, cache token, re-auth on 401.
- Dual interface: programmatic plist-returning functions (hoobs-accessories, hoobs-set-accessory, hoobs-rooms, hoobs-status) and formatted report functions (hoobs-report).
- Register as agent tools so the LLM can query and control home devices via natural language ('turn off the living room lights', 'what's the thermostat set to?', 'lock the front door').
- Config section [hoobs] in config.toml for host/port.

Dependencies: dexador (HTTP), shasht (JSON) — both already in the project.

Security considerations:
- Only allow control operations the user has explicitly enabled via config (e.g. [hoobs] allow-control = true).
- Log all control operations at :info level for audit trail.
- HOOBS is local-network only — no external API calls needed, but egress-policy config should allow the configured HOOBS host.

title: Pushover.net push notification skill
description:
Built-in skill for sending push notifications via the Pushover.net API (https://pushover.net/api). Pushover is a simple REST API for real-time notifications to iOS, Android, and desktop devices.

API: POST https://api.pushover.net/1/messages.json with fields: token (app key), user (user key), message, plus optional title, priority, sound, url, url_title, device, html, timestamp.

Implementation:
- Store credentials in the age-encrypted credential store as 'pushover' with fields :token and :user.
- Daemon-side built-in (like weather, RSS) — runs inside the TCB, not in WASM.
- Dual interface: pushover-send (programmatic, returns plist) and pushover-notify (human-friendly wrapper).
- Register as an agent tool so the LLM can send notifications on request.
- Support priority levels: -2 (lowest) through 2 (emergency, with retry/expire params).
- Useful for: scheduled task failure alerts, RSS new-item notifications, agent-initiated alerts, system health warnings.

Dependencies: dexador (HTTP), shasht (JSON) — both already in the project.

title: BUG/STYLE: H-NOON unused in sunrise-sunset-for-date; solar noon calculation likely missing term
description: skills/builtins/ephemeris.lisp ~line 228: sunrise-sunset-for-date computes H-NOON (hour angle for solar noon) but never uses it. SBCL emits a style-warning. This is likely a correctness bug — the solar noon local mean time calculation (t-noon) should include h-noon like the rise/set calculations include h-rise/h-set: t-noon should be (+ h-noon ra-hours-noon ...) not just (+ ra-hours-noon ...). Compare with the rise/set pattern in the same function.

title: BUG/STYLE: YEAR unused in sunrise-sunset-for-date; day-of-year ignores leap years
description: skills/builtins/ephemeris.lisp: sunrise-sunset-for-date takes YEAR as a parameter but never uses it (style-warning). The underlying issue is that day-of-year ignores leap years — it has no year parameter and uses a fixed day-count table that assumes 28 days in Feb. This means calculations for dates after Feb 28 in leap years will be off by 1 day. Fix: make day-of-year accept year, add leap-year-p check, adjust the offset for month > 2 in leap years.
close_reason: Fixed: YEAR unused warning resolved, h-noon dead code removed in ephemeris.lisp (commit a6609d1)

title: age-encrypt temp files: use mkstemp and 0600 permissions to close security gaps
description:
The pipe-based age-encrypt was replaced with a temp-file approach to fix a pipe buffer deadlock (>64KB payloads). The new approach has two security gaps:

1. Temp files are created with process umask (typically 0644 — world-readable). A local attacker can read plaintext during the encryption window.
2. Temp file names are predictable: get-universal-time (1s resolution) + random 1000000 (non-CSPRNG). A local attacker could race the filename with O_CREAT.
3. SIGKILL between write and delete leaves plaintext in /tmp indefinitely. The old pipe approach had no disk footprint.

Fix:
- Use sb-posix:mkstemp to atomically create temp files with O_EXCL and 0600 permissions, eliminating both the permission window and the name-prediction race.
- Consider ~/.crichton/tmp/ instead of /tmp so temp files are on the same filesystem as the destination (avoids cross-device rename) and under the existing 0700 agent home.
- Document that SIGKILL safety requires tmpfs or accepting the residual risk.

title: Add rss-monitor-stop-many for batch removal without per-stop persist
description:
rss-monitor-stop calls persist-rss-monitors after every single removal. When removing many monitors at once (e.g. cleaning up a bad OPML import), this causes N sequential storage writes and can time out the caller.

Root cause: rss-monitor-stop is designed for single-feed removal and unconditionally persists after each remhash.

Fix: add an rss-monitor-stop-many function (or a :persist nil keyword to rss-monitor-stop) that cancels all scheduler tasks, removes all names from *rss-monitors* under one lock acquisition, then calls persist-rss-monitors exactly once.

title: Deeper skills package refactor: move tool functions out of crichton/skills namespace
description: Why: The current approach of exporting all tool-specific functions from crichton/skills still leaves ~225 exports, far above the ~50 target. Getting to ~50 would require moving domain-specific tool implementations (raindrop, orgmode, books, git, github, hoobs, etc.) into their own sub-packages or making them fully internal to the skills subsystem, with callers going through a generic invoke-skill or a dedicated tool-handler registration rather than calling skills:raindrop-find directly. This is a more invasive architectural change than the simple export removal done in cricht-7kh. What to do: Design a cleaner boundary — likely a tool-handler registry pattern where agent/tools-data.lisp registers handlers via a macro rather than calling skills: functions directly, so the skills package only needs to export the registry protocol (~20 symbols) plus the kv/pipeline/scheduler/monitoring public APIs (~30 symbols).

WASM/CFFI code is inherently non-obvious — the memory management model, ownership semantics, and error conventions all need explanation that cannot be inferred from names alone. What to do: Add docstrings to the ~16 undocumented CFFI helper functions in wasm/engine.lisp. Focus on: memory ownership (who frees what), error return conventions (NULL vs exception vs error code), and the call-with-wasm-* continuation pattern.

This module defines the core LLM abstraction layer — the provider protocol, content block types, and request/response structures. Poor documentation here makes it harder for contributors to understand the extension points and constraints of the LLM integration. What to do: Add docstrings to the ~13 undocumented CLOS methods and class definitions in llm/protocol.lisp. Focus on: what each generic function expects from implementors, slot semantics on content-block classes, and the contract for make-request/parse-response.

title: RSS monitor adaptive backoff and mute
description:
Dead and defunct feeds currently log a warning every poll interval forever. Need per-feed failure tracking with exponential backoff at the scheduler level, auto-disable after N consecutive failures, and an explicit mute/unmute mechanism.

Design sketch:
- Add :consecutive-failures, :muted-until, :user-muted to monitor config plist
- rss-monitor-poll: skip poll if now < muted-until or user-muted; on failure increment counter and set muted-until = now + (interval * 2^failures) capped at ~7 days; on success reset counter and clear muted-until
- After ~10 failures treat feed as dead: push notification, set muted-until far out
- New tool actions: mute_monitor, unmute_monitor (unmute resets failure count)
- Persist mute state through restarts
notes: Pre-validation on import (optional --validate flag) would catch dead feeds at import time, reducing the need for auto-disable. The adaptive backoff and mute features are still useful for feeds that go dead post-registration. Cost of pre-validation: serialises N HTTP fetches into the import path — consider making it opt-in.
close_reason: Exponential backoff (interval * 2^n, 7-day cap) in rss-monitor-poll; dead-feed notification at 10 failures; mute/unmute tool actions; backoff state persisted/restored across restarts.

title: Amp orchestrator: dual interface at tool level — add structured JSON-returning tool variants alongside existing formatted-text tools so the agent can chain results programmatically
Refactor wasm/engine.lisp macros: unify lifecycle, improve testability
description: The three macros in wasm/engine.lisp (with-wasm-simple-module, with-wasm-host-environment, defhost-callback) are overly complex with deep nesting (8+ levels), code duplication, and poor testability. Refactor into composable continuation-passing functions with thin macro wrappers.
design:
Three-part refactoring plan for wasm/engine.lisp macro complexity:

**1. Unify with-wasm-simple-module and with-wasm-host-environment (biggest win)**

Both macros share ~80% structure (engine/store creation, WAT compilation, module compilation, instantiation, cleanup). Extract continuation-passing helper functions:

- `call-with-wasm-bytes (source-type source k)` — handles :WAT vs :WASM source, calls K with (ptr len). Eliminates the duplicated :WAT/:WASM branches inside with-wasm-host-environment.
- `call-with-compiled-module (engine ptr len k)` — handles module-ptr allocation, wasmtime-module-new, deletion. Calls K with module.
- `call-with-instantiated-instance (context module k &key linker)` — handles instance + trap-ptr foreign objects, calls the right instantiate API (wasmtime-instance-new vs wasmtime-linker-instantiate) based on whether linker is provided.
- `call-with-wasm-environment (source-type source fn &key host-fn-registrar)` — composes the above three. The single canonical lifecycle function.

The existing macros become trivial 2-line wrappers:
  (defmacro with-wasm-simple-module ((ctx inst) wat &body body)
    `(call-with-wasm-environment :wat ,wat (lambda (,ctx ,inst) ,@body)))
  (defmacro with-wasm-host-environment ((ctx inst) (src-type src) &body body)
    `(call-with-wasm-environment ,src-type ,src (lambda (,ctx ,inst) ,@body)
       :host-fn-registrar #'register-standard-host-fns))

**2. Make defhost-callback generate a testable impl function**

Currently the only callable thing is the CFFI callback — untestable from Lisp. Refactor defhost-callback to emit two definitions:
- `(defun <name>-impl (caller results nresults ...args...) ...body...)` — the real logic, testable from pure Lisp.
- `(cffi:defcallback <name> ...)` — thin wrapper that validates nargs, extracts i32 args, delegates to -impl, catches errors.

**3. Deduplicate call-i32-export / call-export-by-name**

These are near-identical. Keep call-export-by-name (used by JSON ABI code), make call-i32-export a thin alias or delete it and update its 3 call sites.

**Pointer lifetime safety note:** all continuation-passing helpers must keep foreign pointers within their dynamic extent. The K functions must not escape pointers into slots or return values.

title: DSL macros to reduce code duplication across codebase
description:
## Summary

Systematic analysis of the Crichton Lisp codebase reveals 8 major repeated code patterns ("rhyming" code) that could be collapsed into domain-specific macros.  The macros would make the code read more like a specification of intent than plumbing.

## Proposed Macros

### 1. define-tool (agent/tools.lisp) — 13 instances
Wraps the register-tool + make-properties + make-json-schema + hget boilerplate into a declarative form.

Before:
  (defun register-weather-tool ()
    (multiple-value-bind (props required)
        (make-properties '("city" "string" "desc"))
      (register-tool "weather" "desc"
        (make-json-schema :type "object" :properties props :required required)
        (lambda (input) (let ((city (hget input "city"))) ...)))))

After:
  (define-tool weather (:description "desc")
    ((city "string" "desc"))
    ...)

### 2. guarded / guarded-steps (daemon/lifecycle.lisp) — 14 instances
Wraps the handler-case + log:warn pattern used in start-daemon (8x) and stop-daemon (6x).

Before: (handler-case (X) (error (c) (log:warn "Y failed: ~A" c)))
After:  (guarded ("Y failed") (X))
Bulk:   (guarded-steps ("scheduler" (start-scheduler)) ("rpc" (start-rpc)) ...)

### 3. with-standard-host-fns (wasm/engine.lisp) — 4 identical copies
The same ~60-line block of 8 host function registrations (log, kv_get, kv_set, kv_delete, kv_exists, kv_list, http_request, get_secret) + functype creation + cleanup is duplicated verbatim in run-wasm-with-host-fns, run-wasm-bytes-with-host-fns, run-wasm-json-call, run-wasm-bytes-json-call.  Extract into a macro that creates, registers, and cleans up all functypes.

### 4. defhost-callback (wasm/engine.lisp) — 6 callbacks
Every cffi:defcallback follows the same template: arg-count validation, arg extraction via wasmtime-val-i32, handler-case wrapping, null-pointer return.  A macro eliminates the ceremony.

### 5. string-case (tools, rpc-server, runner) — 5+ dispatch sites
Replace (cond ((string-equal x "a") ...) ((string-equal x "b") ...) (t ...)) with a case-like form: (string-case x ("a" ...) ("b" ...) (otherwise ...))

### 6. Shared plist<->JSON codecs — duplicated in 2-3 packages
plist-to-json-bytes, json-bytes-to-plist, safe-intern-keyword are copy-pasted verbatim between credentials/backend-age-file.lisp and sessions/store.lisp.  Extract to a shared util module.

### 7. iso8601-now — duplicated in 3 packages
Identical function in storage/store.lisp, sessions/store.lisp, logging/logger.lisp.  Extract to shared util.

### 8. delete-file-if-exists — 6 locations
(when (probe-file path) (delete-file path)) used in lifecycle, rpc-server, runner-client, storage, credentials, sessions.  Trivial utility function or macro.

## Impact Estimate

Total lines of duplication that would be eliminated: ~800-1000 lines across the codebase.  Most impactful are #3 (4x60 = 240 lines of WASM boilerplate) and #1 (13 tool registrations averaging 25 lines each = 325 lines of schema boilerplate).

## Implementation Order

1. Shared utilities first (#6, #7, #8) — zero risk, pure dedup
2. guarded-steps (#2) — high value, simple macro
3. string-case (#5) — small macro, widely useful
4. define-tool (#1) — highest line savings
5. with-standard-host-fns (#3) — biggest single-file impact
6. defhost-callback (#4) — CFFI-adjacent, test carefully
notes:
All 8 macros/utilities implemented:
#1 define-tool: 13 tools converted (agent/tools.lisp)
#2 guarded: 17 handler-case blocks replaced (daemon/lifecycle.lisp, −27 lines)
#3 with-wasm-host-environment: 4 functions collapsed to 3-line wrappers (wasm/engine.lisp, −315 lines)
#4 defhost-callback: 8 callbacks deduplicated (wasm/engine.lisp)
#5 string-case: macro + 2 dispatch sites converted (config/util.lisp, rpc-server, runner)
#6 plist/JSON codecs: centralized in config/util.lisp, 4 duplicates removed
#7 iso8601-now: centralized, 4 duplicates removed
#8 delete-file-if-exists: centralized, ~11 call sites updated
Total: ~400 lines of duplication eliminated.  Zero STYLE-WARNINGs.  Documented in DEVELOPER.org.

title: Prefer defclass over defstruct for hot-reload safety
description:
**Context:**
SBCL rejects incompatible struct redefinitions during hot-reload, but CLOS classes update existing instances automatically via update-instance-for-redefined-class.

**History:**
- cricht-rcl: Converted defstructs to defclasses across the codebase
- cricht-6d7: Skill registry inadvertently reintroduced skill-entry as defstruct
- This bead: Converted skill-entry to defclass

**Policy:**
**Always use defclass instead of defstruct** for evolving data types.

**Rationale:**
- Hot-reload safety: Class redefinitions update existing instances
- Development workflow: Enables iterative development without daemon restart
- Production: No impact (classes vs structs both efficient)

**When to use defstruct:**
Only for truly fixed-layout, performance-critical types that will never change. In practice: almost never.

**How to enforce:**
- Code review: Flag new defstruct uses
- Documentation: Note in AGENTS.org and DEVELOPER.org
- CI lint: Consider adding defstruct detection (future)

**See also:**
- cricht-rcl (original conversion)
- AGENTS.org section on hot-loading

title: COMPILER NOTE: unreachable code in toml-value-to-lisp
description: config/loader.lisp: SBCL emits a note about unreachable code in toml-value-to-lisp. The cond has branches for hash-table-p, eq cl-toml:true, eq cl-toml:false, stringp, and a catch-all t. SBCL can prove the final branch is unreachable after type inference. Fix: rewrite using typecase for hash-table and string, with a nested cond for boolean symbols, to eliminate the dead code note and improve clarity.

title: STYLE: host callbacks declare RESULTS ignored but write through it

SBCL emits 7 style-warnings total. Fix: remove RESULTS from the ignore declaration in each callback. Optionally add nresults >= 1 guard.

title: STYLE: unused parameter ENGINE in define-host-fn

close_reason: Fixed: added (declare (ignore engine)) to define-host-fn (commit e29fdb3)

title: Revisit CLI architecture: standalone vs daemon-backed commands
description:
The need to add console logging suppression for standalone CLI commands (chat, weather, etc.) raises questions about the current architecture. Currently some commands talk to the daemon via Swank (stop, status, eval) while others run as completely separate instances (chat, weather, cred, doctor).

Consider consolidating to have more commands talk to the daemon rather than running standalone instances. This would:
- Avoid logging configuration duplication/issues
- Potentially share state and resources more efficiently
- Simplify the mental model

Trade-offs to evaluate:
- Startup latency for simple commands
- Dependency on daemon being running
- Complexity of remote invocation vs direct calls
- Session isolation vs state sharing

This is marked P3 as it's not urgent, but worth revisiting once the core functionality is stable.

title: Weather skill bypasses egress control
description:
skills/builtins/weather.lisp makes direct HTTP calls via dexador without going through an allowlist check. Not critical (daemon-side built-in, not WASM) but inconsistent with the spec's egress control philosophy.

Notes: Built-in skills are part of the TCB so this is a consistency issue, not a vulnerability.
close_reason: Fixed: check-weather-egress added; blocks request if api.weather.gc.ca not in [network] allowed_builtin_http_domains (commit 33dd5ca)

title: Version information visibility
description:
Version info should be prominent and accessible in multiple contexts: Swank REPL banner on connect, daemon startup log message, system-info skill output, and the agent's own self-knowledge (system prompt or introspectable). Currently only available via 'crichton version' CLI command. Include git commit hash at build time if possible.

Notes: Troubleshooting is difficult without visible version. Consider embedding git rev via save-lisp-and-die build step.

This causes 7 tests to fail with 'ReferenceError: HtmlSanitizer is not defined'. Need to add HtmlSanitizer to the test setup files or mock it.

description: getAllOfficeHours(), getAllHiddenMeetings(), and getAllVisibleCourses() return result[key] || {} without checking the type. If storage is corrupted (string/array), callers crash on property assignment. Should use _isPlainObject() guard.

value: Codebase health report (2026-05-14): Grade B+. 93 Lisp files, ~21,600 LOC, 16 packages, 9 test suites (~1,900 LOC test code). All health issues resolved as of 2026-05-14: (1) crichton/skills now exports ~90 symbols (down from 311); ~130 domain-tool symbols moved to :intern, imported explicitly by crichton/agent and crichton/daemon; (2) llm/protocol.lisp docstring coverage raised to 100%; (3) agent/tools-data.lisp (666 LOC) split into 9 focused files; (4) storage/store.lisp and sessions/store.lisp now have unit tests (test-storage.lisp, test-sessions.lisp); (5) wasm/engine.lisp docstring coverage raised to 100% with defhost-callback docstring support.

Prefer small, targeted Lisp edits over large rewrites. Write one form at a time and hot-load to verify before moving on. Do not count parens manually or use Python scripts to audit balance — that is a sign the edit was too large. Use the SLIME REPL (port 4005) to validate forms incrementally.

Bump the patch version (z in x.y.z) with every bug fix. Multiple commits fixing the same underlying issue count as one bump. Feature/refactor work bumps minor (y). Both config/loader.lisp (*crichton-version*) and crichton.asd (:version) must be updated together.
